text: Spatial charting of single cell transcriptomes in tissues. Runmin Wei, Siyuan He, Shanshan Bai, Emi Sei, Min Hu, Alastair Thompson, Ken Chen, Savitri Krishnamurthy, Nicholas E. Navin. Published in final edited form as: Nat Biotechnol. 2022 August; 40(8): 1190-1199. doi:10.1038/s41587-022-01233-1. Abstract: Single cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) methods can profile the transcriptomes of single cells but cannot preserve spatial information. Conversely, spatial transcriptomics (ST) assays can profile spatial regions in tissue sections, but do not have single cell genomic resolution. Here, we developed a computational approach called CellTrek that combines these two datasets to achieve single cell spatial mapping. We benchmarked CellTrek using a simulation study and two in situ datasets. We then applied CellTrek to reconstruct cellular spatial structures in existing datasets from normal mouse brain and kidney tissues. We also performed scRNA-seq and ST experiments on two ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) tissues and applied CellTrek to identify tumor subclones that were restricted to different ducts, and specific T cell states adjacent to the tumor areas. Our data shows that CellTrek can accurately map single cells in diverse tissue types to resolve their spatial organization.

Background: scRNA-seq methods have greatly expanded our understanding of gene expression programs of diverse cell types and their role in development and disease. However, scRNA-seq inherently loses cellular spatial information during tissue dissociation, which is critical for understanding cellular microenvironment and cell-cell interactions. While spatial sequencing methods, including ST and Slide-seq, can spatially profile gene expression across tissue sections, they are limited to measuring small regions with mixtures of cells and cannot easily provide single cell information. Computational deconvolution approaches (e.g., cell2location, RCTD) have been designed to deconvolute ST spots into proportions of different cell types. However, these methods are limited to inferring only cell type proportions for each spot, cannot achieve single cell resolution, have limited ability to resolve cell states, and most can only predict categorical labels, not continuous cell information (e.g., lineage trajectories, gene signatures) at spatial resolution. Here we introduce CellTrek, a computational toolkit that can directly map single cells back to their spatial coordinates in tissue sections based on scRNA-seq and ST data. This provides a new paradigm distinct from ST deconvolution, enabling flexible and direct investigation of single cell data with spatial topography. The CellTrek toolkit also provides two downstream analysis modules: SColoc for spatial colocalization analysis and SCoexp for spatial co-expression analysis.

section: Results Part 1: Overview of CellTrek Toolkit and Methods
text: Overview: CellTrek first integrates and co-embeds ST and scRNA-seq data into a shared feature space. Using the ST data, CellTrek trains a multivariate random forests (RF) model to predict spatial coordinates using shared dimension reduction features. A spatial non-linear interpolation on ST data is introduced to augment spatial resolution. The trained model is then applied to the co-embedded data to derive an RF-distance matrix which measures expression similarities between ST spots and single cells supervised by spatial coordinates. Based on the RF-distance matrix, CellTrek produces a sparse spot-cell graph using mutual nearest neighbors (MNN) after thresholding. Finally, CellTrek transfers spatial coordinates for cells from their neighbor spots. To improve compatibility, CellTrek can accept any cell-location probability/distance matrix from other methods (e.g., novoSpaRc) as input. A graphical user interface (GUI) is provided for interactive visualization. Downstream Modules: 1) SColoc summarizes the CellTrek result into a graph abstraction. Three approaches are provided to calculate spatial dissimilarity between cell types: Kullback-Leibler divergence (KL), Delaunay triangulation (DT), and K-nearest neighbor distance (KD). Based on the dissimilarity matrix, SColoc can construct a minimum spanning tree (MST) representing simplified spatial cellular proximity. Steps are iterated on bootstrapping samples to generate consensus matrices. 2) SCoexp leverages CellTrek coordinates to detect co-expression gene modules within cells of interest. It calculates a spatial kernel weight matrix, then a spatial-weighted gene co-expression matrix. It uses consensus clustering (CC) or weighted correlation network analysis (WGCNA) to identify gene modules. Module scores can be calculated and their spatial organization investigated.

Benchmarking: We exploited three spatial datasets: 1) a simulated scRNA-seq dataset with customized spatial patterns, 2) a fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH)-based single cell dataset of the Drosophila embryo, and 3) a seqFISH dataset of mouse embryo. We generated three corresponding ST datasets with each spot aggregating the five spatially nearest cells. We applied CellTrek and compared it to NVSP-CellTrek (which uses novoSpaRc to calculate a cell-spatial probability matrix, then leverages CellTrek) and Seurat coordinate transfer (SrtCT). Both CellTrek and NVSP-CellTrek reconstructed the original spatial pattern of the simulated data, while SrtCT could not accurately map the cells. CellTrek mapped more cells with higher spatial density. Quantitative evaluation using KL-divergence showed CellTrek and NVSP-CellTrek achieved good performance with low KL-divergences, while SrtCT showed much higher discrepancies. In the Drosophila embryo data, CellTrek accurately reconstructed the original spatial layout with the lowest KL-divergence. In the mouse embryo data, CellTrek and NVSP-CellTrek accurately reconstructed the original spatial structure. Trajectory analysis on gut tube cells showed pseudotime reflected the spatial developing pattern along the anterior-posterior axis. Robustness Assessment: We assessed CellTrek on simulated data under three settings: 1) read counts, 2) spatial randomness, and 3) tissue densities. Evaluation used KL-divergence and Pearson's correlation between CellTrek map and reference coordinates. CellTrek achieved good spatial reconstruction performance across simulations. Increasing spatial randomness decreased performance, while decreasing read counts or spot/cell density resulted in sparse cellular maps. Overall, CellTrek is robust for single cell spatial mapping under different experimental conditions.

section: Results Part 3: Application to Mouse Brain and Kidney Tissues
text: Mouse Brain: We applied CellTrek to public mouse brain scRNA-seq (Smart-seq2) and ST (Visium) datasets. CellTrek reconstructed a clear layer structure of laminar excitatory neuron subtypes (L2/3 IT, L4, L5 IT, L6 IT, L6 CT, L6b) matching the cerebral cortex structure. NVSP-CellTrek showed a similar trend but with sparser mapping. SrtCT failed to accurately project cell locations. KL-divergence using Seurat label transfer (SrtLT) as reference showed CellTrek had the lowest divergence. CellTrek further uncovered topological patterns of cell states within the same cell type (e.g., five expression states within L5 IT cells forming a refined sub-layer architecture). SColoc analysis using KL-based MST consensus graph showed glutamatergic neuron cell types constructed a linear backbone in the order of the layer structures. Spatial K-distance to L2/3 IT cells showed a significant increasing trend. SCoexp identified two co-expression modules (K1, K2) within L5 IT cells with different functional enrichments and spatial distributions (outer vs. inner layer). Mouse Kidney: CellTrek accurately reconstructed cellular spatial structures with distinct cell types in different histological zones (cortex, outer medulla, inner medulla). Trajectory analysis of ProxTub and DistTub cells showed continuous spatial trajectories consistent with known anatomy. SColoc graph identified ProxTub as a hub. CellTrek recapitulated experimental zonal dissection information. SCoexp identified two spatial co-expression modules (K1, K2) in DistTub cells, enriched in DCT (cortex) and DST (medulla) pathways respectively, matching anatomic localizations. Cell-cell interaction analysis (CellChat) filtered by the SColoc graph provided a more concise and specific set of interactions, including known Vegfa-Flt1/Kdr interaction.

section: Results Part 4: Application to Human DCIS Breast Cancer Samples
text: DCIS1 (Pure DCIS): We applied scRNA-seq and ST to a DCIS sample. CopyKAT inferred copy number profiles, identifying three major tumor subclones (clone1-3) with distinct alterations and a phylogenetic tree. Hallmark GSEA identified common and subclone-specific pathways. CellTrek mapped most tumor cells to DCIS regions on H&E slide. Different tumor subclones mapped to different ductal regions, showing spatial intratumor heterogeneity. Clonal diversity (Shannon index) varied across ducts. SCoexp identified three spatial co-expression modules (K1, K2, K3) associated with different clones and functions (e.g., K3 with proliferation, located near duct peripheries). DCIS2 (Synchronous DCIS-IDC): We profiled another DCIS sample. CellTrek mapped aneuploid cells to DCIS regions and immune cells to areas surrounding ducts and stroma. Immune cell aggregations outside ducts suggested tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS), supported by ST spot-level TLS scores correlating with charted immune cell counts. T cell analysis revealed six states; Tregs and exhausted T cells were proximal to tumor cells. A spatial graph showed cells from same lineages colocalized. T cell exhaustion scores were higher proximal to tumor. Myeloid cell analysis identified four states. cDCs were proximal to tumor. Macrophage SCoexp analysis identified gene modules with opposite spatial patterns. Orthogonal Validation: RNAscope immunofluorescence on DCIS2 and a third sample (DCIS3) validated spatial distribution: ERBB2 high in tumor areas; immunosuppressive T cell markers (CTLA4, FOXP3) high near DCIS; immune cell markers (MS4A1, CD68, CD1C) near ducts, suggesting TLS. Fewer immune cells were in normal lobular areas.

Discussion: CellTrek provides a new paradigm that directly projects single cells to spatial coordinates, taking full advantage of scRNA-seq data. Advantages: 1) Flexible investigation of any single-cell feature (types/states, pseudotime) spatially; 2) Can take any cell-location probability matrix as input; 3) Uses metric learning and non-linear interpolation for accurate, high-resolution charting; 4) Applicable to higher spatial-resolution technologies. We benchmarked CellTrek and demonstrated its capability in mouse brain and kidney. In DCIS, CellTrek delineated spatial distribution of tumor subclones and tumor-immune microenvironment. Limitations: 1) Can have sparse cell mapping in some areas (solutions: higher cellular density ST, more cells, integrate datasets). 2) Requires ST spots with relatively high cell purities; increasing spatial randomness (decreasing purity) can reduce performance. 3) Risk of over-interpretation; orthogonal validation is recommended. Future directions: Include image recognition or deep learning for cell segmentation; adapt for epigenome data (e.g., scATAC-seq). Methods: CellTrek Toolkit. Co-embedding: Uses a reference-based co-embedding approach from Seurat with scRNA-seq as reference, ST as query. Model Training: Trains a multivariate RF model on ST data using rfsrc with formula: $$[X, Y]\sim PCs$$, where X and Y are spatial coordinates, PCs are top principal components (default=30). Interpolation: Introduces a non-linear 2D interpolation to augment ST spots. Distance: Applies trained RF to co-embedded data to produce RF-distance matrix: $$RF\,distance = \frac{\#\,edges\,to\,the\,immediate\,shared\,ancestor}{\#\,edges\,to\,the\,tree\,root}$$.

section: Methods Part 2: SColoc, SCoexp, and Data Analysis
text: SColoc: Provides three approaches: 1) KL-divergence: Calculates 2D grid kernel density for each cell type, then KL-divergence between pairs. Good for global closeness. 2) Delaunay triangulation (DT): Builds 2D DT network, filters long edges, calculates neighboring cell counts, then log odds ratio between cell types. Good for local structures. 3) K-nearest neighbor distance (KD). Steps are performed on bootstrap samples. Produces bootstrap closeness matrix and MST consensus matrix for graph visualization. SCoexp: For cells of interest, calculates spatial distance matrix, converts to spatial kernel matrix W using RBF. Calculates spatial-weighted gene cross-correlation matrix: $$W\,Cor = \frac{X^{T} W X}{\sqrt{\operatorname{diag}\left(X^{T} W X\right)\operatorname{diag}\left(X^{T} W X\right)^{T}}},$$ where X is gene expression matrix. Uses consensus clustering (CC) or WGCNA to identify gene modules. Adds module activity scores. Data Acquisition and Analysis: Mouse brain, hippocampus, and kidney data were obtained from public sources and analyzed with standard Seurat workflows. DCIS Tissue Collection and Sequencing: Two DCIS samples collected with IRB approval. scRNA-seq: 10X Genomics 3' chemistry. ST: Visium spatial gene expression. RNAscope: Multiplex fluorescent assay with probes for ERBB2, CTLA4, FOXP3, immune markers etc. DCIS Data Analysis: scRNA-seq processed with CellRanger and Seurat. Copy number inferred with CopyKAT. Trajectory with Monocle2. ST data processed with SpaceRanger and Seurat. TLS score calculated from 12-gene signature. Benchmarking Simulations: Used Splatter to simulate scRNA-seq data. ST spots generated by aggregating nearest cells. Simulated different conditions: read count dropouts, spatial randomness (Gaussian noise on coordinates), tissue densities (spatial down-sampling). Permutation tests for null distributions.

Acknowledgements: Supported by NIH grants, CPRIT, Chan-Zuckerberg Initiative, and MD Anderson core facilities. Data and Software Availability: The scRNA-seq and ST data were submitted to GEO: GSE181254. The CellTrek software toolkit is available at GitHub: https://github.com/navinlabcode/CellTrek. Key References: 1. Ståhl et al. Visualization and analysis of gene expression in tissue sections by spatial transcriptomics. Science 2016. 2. Rodriques et al. Slide-seq: A scalable technology for measuring genome-wide expression at high spatial resolution. Science 2019. 3. Cable et al. Robust decomposition of cell type mixtures in spatial transcriptomics. Nat Biotechnol 2021. 4. Moncada et al. Integrating microarray-based spatial transcriptomics and single-cell RNA-seq reveals tissue architecture in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinomas. Nat Biotechnol 2020. 5. Tasic et al. Adult mouse cortical cell taxonomy revealed by single cell transcriptomics. Nat Neurosci 2016. 6. Gao et al. Delineating copy number and clonal substructure in human tumors from single-cell transcriptomes. Nat Biotechnol 2021. 7. Jin et al. Inference and analysis of cell-cell communication using CellChat. Nat Commun 2021.
